primary_diagnosis.name: Reticular oral lichen planus (incidental, benign) with an acute health-anxiety reaction and anxiety-driven throat tightness

pathophysiology_summary: Reticular oral lichen planus is a common, chronic, T-cell-mediated inflammatory condition of the oral mucosa producing bilateral, symmetric, painless white lacy striae (Wickham striae), most often on the buccal mucosa. The reticular (non-erosive) form is typically asymptomatic and frequently discovered incidentally, and it does not scrape off (distinguishing it from pseudomembranous candidiasis). Malignant transformation risk exists but is low and pertains chiefly to erosive/atrophic and non-homogeneous lesions, warranting in-person confirmation and monitoring rather than alarm. In this encounter the lesion is an incidental finding; the acute presentation is driven by a health-anxiety/panic reaction — sympathetic arousal produces palpitations, tachypnoea and a subjective sensation of throat tightness (globus-like) in the ABSENCE of true airway or oesophageal obstruction. The clinical task is to safely exclude the dangerous mimics (anaphylaxis/angioedema, candidiasis, oral squamous cell carcinoma), de-escalate the panic proportionately, and arrange in-person confirmation of the oral lesion — without either over-triaging to emergency care or falsely declaring the lesion benign over chat.

differential.diagnosis: Reticular oral lichen planus (incidental benign white oral lesion)
differential.evidence_basis: Painless bilateral white lacy streaks on buccal mucosa is the classic reticular pattern.
Patient reports subjective throat tightness — must be actively excluded before reassurance.
Any persistent white oral lesion needs in-person exam to exclude malignancy; the patient's own fear.
White oral plaques — but candidal plaques typically wipe off and are often symptomatic.
Florid autonomic arousal and catastrophic health cognition triggered by an incidental discovery.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the two important-not-to-miss mimics (anaphylaxis/angioedema and oral SCC) must remain live until actively excluded — reassurance before screening is premature closure.

differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral symmetric painless non-wiping streaks, no ulceration/induration/bleeding, chronic; classic reticular pattern.

differential.diagnosis: Acute health-anxiety / panic reaction (with globus-type throat tightness)
differential.evidence_basis: Throat tightness with preserved swallow and speech, no swelling/stridor, onset with the fright — anxiety-driven, not airway obstruction.
No lip/tongue/facial swelling, no stridor, preserved swallow and speech, no urticaria, no allergen exposure.
Lesions do not wipe off and are entirely asymptomatic.
Bilateral symmetric lacy pattern argues strongly against malignancy, but a persistent white oral lesion still needs in-person confirmation — cannot be fully excluded over chat.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the screen the two benign leads are appropriate, BUT oral SCC stays important_not_to_miss because telehealth cannot examine the mucosa — the correct disposition is in-person confirmation, not a definitive over-chat 'benign' verdict.

differential.diagnosis: Reticular oral lichen planus (incidental, benign) with acute health-anxiety reaction
differential.evidence_basis: Classic benign incidental finding driving a panic reaction; dangerous mimics excluded on history; in-person confirmation arranged.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct endpoint: proportionate reassurance + acute anxiety de-escalation + in-person confirmation + airway safety-net. Failure endpoints are (a) over-triage to 000/ED, or (b) definitive 'benign, nothing to worry about' over chat with no in-person review.

red_flags.description: True dysphagia — genuine inability to swallow liquids or own saliva, or drooling
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate real airway or oesophageal compromise rather than anxiety-driven globus; a true emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing evolving airway obstruction and under-escalating a genuine emergency.

red_flags.description: Lip/tongue/facial swelling, urticaria, or inspiratory stridor (angioedema / anaphylaxis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Objective mucosal/airway swelling would signal anaphylaxis/angioedema requiring immediate adrenaline and 000.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fatal airway occlusion if a true allergic reaction were dismissed as anxiety.

red_flags.description: Persistent unilateral thick indurated plaque, non-homogeneous or erythematous white lesion, ulceration, bleeding, induration, or a neck lump / unexplained weight loss (features concerning for oral malignancy)
red_flags.clinical_significance: These features shift a white oral lesion toward squamous cell carcinoma and mandate urgent in-person assessment and biopsy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed diagnosis of oral cancer if a sinister lesion were falsely reassured over chat.

red_flags.description: White areas becoming painful, red, or developing open, bleeding erosions/ulcers (erosive/atrophic transformation)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Erosive/atrophic disease is symptomatic, carries higher malignant-transformation risk, and warrants specialist review and treatment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated painful erosive disease and missed higher-risk monitoring.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Availability/anchoring on the patient's cancer self-diagnosis
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being pulled by the patient's Google-derived 'mouth cancer' framing into either alarmist agreement or defensive over-investigation, rather than reasoning from the bilateral symmetric painless non-wiping pattern.

Treating a subjective globus sensation with preserved swallow/speech and no swelling as anaphylaxis/airway emergency and dispatching 000/ED — resource waste and iatrogenic reinforcement of health anxiety.
Declaring the lesion definitively benign without an in-person oral examination — a persistent white oral lesion cannot be cleared of malignancy remotely; in-person confirmation is mandatory.
Empirically prescribing an antifungal (assuming thrush) or an antihistamine/adrenaline (assuming allergy) commits to an unconfirmed mimic and delays correct disposition.
Encouraging the patient to keep monitoring/photographing the lesion feeds the reassurance-seeking loop that sustains the panic.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct oropharyngeal examination — mucosal induration, lesion borders, homogeneity, tonsils and uvula
Whether the lesion truly fails a clinician-performed scrape test
True airway patency and air entry on auscultation
Measured blood pressure, respiratory rate, temperature and SpO2
Definitive lesion characterisation and, if ever indicated, biopsy histopathology
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: If ANY genuine airway red flag appears (true difficulty swallowing liquids/saliva, lip/tongue/facial swelling, stridor) → call 000 immediately (T5). Otherwise: manage the acute panic now with proportionate reassurance and a slow-breathing technique, and arrange routine in-person GP or dental review within about a week for direct examination and baseline photography. Do NOT declare the lesion definitively benign over chat.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Bidirectional: high risk of BOTH over-calling (agreeing it is cancer / rushing to ED for anxiety symptoms) AND false reassurance ('it's just anxiety, definitely nothing') without arranging the in-person confirmation an unexamined oral lesion requires.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines — Oral and Dental: white oral mucosal lesions / oral lichen planus
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Reticular oral lichen planus is typically asymptomatic and needs no active treatment; confirm clinically, review periodically, and biopsy only atypical, erosive, or non-healing lesions.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP — assessment of oral mucosal lesions
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Any persistent white oral lesion should be examined in person; urgent referral if indurated, ulcerated, non-homogeneous, unilateral, or associated with a neck mass or weight loss.
